docs.title: Who this is for
docs.text: Instructors, course coordinators, and institutions, especially community colleges, who want proven, NSF-funded data science courses and modules they can stand up in a couple of hours and adapt to their students. If you only need computing infrastructure (a JupyterHub), see Infrastructure & Tools - Cal-ICOR and Cloudbank can provision a hub independently of curriculum adoption.

docs.title: UC Berkeley Courses
docs.text:
• Data 6: Computational Thinking with Data Science and Society

    Interdisciplinary introduction with course notes and grading infrastructure. Full adoption package.

    Adopt Data 6

  • Data 8: Foundations of Data Science

    Inference, computing, and statistics for first-year students. Full adoption package with two versions.

    Adopt Data 8

  • Data 100: Principles and Techniques of Data Science

    Adoption materials are in progress, resources are available now.

    Data 100 resources

docs.title: Modules
docs.text:
Standalone Jupyter notebooks you can drop into an existing course - no full course adoption required.

  • Cal-ICOR Modules

    Computational notebooks for courses across California higher education. Contribute or adopt notebooks.

    Cal-ICOR

  • Data Science Modules

    Multidisciplinary Jupyter notebooks from UC Berkeley DSUS - ethnic studies, AI, calculus, chemistry, and more.

    Data Science Modules

docs.title: Partner Courses
docs.text:
Courses developed outside UC Berkeley that share the same open-source infrastructure and adoption workflow.

  • El Camino College CSCI 9: Practical Data Science

    A collaboration with UC Berkeley Data Science Modules. Adoption materials are in progress, resources are available now.

    CSCI 9 resources

docs.title: About
docs.text: This site brings courses, modules, and infrastructure developed by UC Berkeley and partner institutions (notebooks, solutions, slides, worksheets, Canvas templates, and autograding) to instructors and institutions everywhere.

docs.title: About
docs.text: The central home for adopting data science curriculum and computing infrastructure.

docs.title: Why adopt
docs.text:
• Complete course and module packages: notebooks, slides, and assignments ready to use.
  • Proven pedagogy: refined over many semesters at UC Berkeley and partner institutions including El Camino College.
  • Full support: access to grading infrastructure, Canvas templates, and our team.
  • Computing infrastructure: hub provisioning through Cal-ICOR (California) and Cloudbank (nationwide), even without adopting curriculum.
  • Open and adaptable: modify the materials to fit your institution (within each course's or module's license).
  • NSF-funded: supported by the National Science Foundation and partners such as Cloudbank and the California Learning Lab.

docs.title: Acknowledgments
docs.text: This work is supported by the U.S. National Science Foundation and partners including Cloudbank and the California Learning Lab. Many faculty, instructors, and teaching assistants at UC Berkeley and partner institutions have contributed to these courses and modules across the years.

docs.title: Contact
docs.text: Questions about adopting? Email ds-help@berkeley.edu.

docs.title: Contact
docs.text: Email ds-help@berkeley.edu and we'll point you to the right person. For common issues, see Support and the FAQ.

docs.title: Contact
docs.text: Questions about adopting? We're here to help.

docs.title: Courses
docs.text: Browse courses available for adoption and follow each course's page for repositories, hub, and license details.

docs.title: UC Berkeley Courses
docs.text:
Each course below follows the same adoption workflow; the course page fills in the specific repositories, Canvas template, hub, license, and any course-specific notes.

  • Data 6: Computational Thinking with Data Science and Society

    Full adoption package. Interdisciplinary introduction with course notes and grading infrastructure.

  • Data 8: Foundations of Data Science

    Full adoption package. Inference, computing, and statistics for first-year students. Two adoption versions; split textbook / materials licensing.

  • Data 100: Principles and Techniques of Data Science

    Adoption materials in progress. Existing resources available now.

docs.title: Partner Courses
docs.text:
Courses developed outside UC Berkeley that share the same open-source infrastructure and adoption workflow.

  • El Camino College CSCI 9: Practical Data Science

    Not a UC Berkeley course. A collaboration with UC Berkeley Data Science Modules. Adoption materials in progress. Existing resources available now.

docs.title: ECC CSCI 9
docs.text:
Not a UC Berkeley course

This course originates from El Camino College, not UC Berkeley. It is a collaboration with UC Berkeley Data Science Modules and is included here because it uses the same open-source infrastructure and adoption workflow as the Berkeley courses on this site.

Adoption materials in progress

A full adoption package is actively being developed. Public student materials and instructor resources are available now; the standardized adoption workflow will apply once the package is complete.

docs.title: El Camino College CSCI 9: Practical Data Science
Materials
docs.text:
• CSCI 9 Textbook: ds-modules.github.io/ecc-cs9-textbook
  • Data Science Modules: ds-modules.github.io/ecc-textbook
  • Browse notebooks in your browser: El Camino College CSCI 9: Practical Data Science xeus-lite
  • Public student materials: eccdatascience/csci9-sp26-student
  • Private instructor repo (solutions and course-development materials): ds-modules/cs-9-dev. To request access, complete the CSCI 9 Instructor Interest Form.

docs.title: Explore the materials in your browser
docs.text:
The full course calendar is also available as an in-browser notebook preview, with links to slides, worksheets, and videos where available. It runs the notebooks directly in your browser (xeus-lite), so it is handy for previewing the materials. It is not meant for active classroom use, but it is a quick way to explore what the course covers.

Browse El Camino College CSCI 9: Practical Data Science notebooks in your browser

docs.title: Get notified
docs.text: Interested in adopting this course? Complete the CSCI 9 Instructor Interest Form or email ds-help@berkeley.edu and we'll help you get started.

docs.title: Data 100
docs.text:
Adoption materials in progress

A full Data 100 adoption package is actively being developed. Public student materials and instructor resources are available now; the standardized adoption workflow will apply once the package is complete.

Data 100 is UC Berkeley's upper-division "Principles and Techniques of Data Science" course, building on Data 8 with deeper coverage of the data science lifecycle, modeling, and machine learning.

docs.title: Data 100: Principles and Techniques of Data Science
Materials
docs.text:
• Course site: ds100.org
  • Public student materials: DS-100/sp25-student
  • Private instructor repo (solutions and instructor materials): DS-100/materials-ptds-private — email ds-help@berkeley.edu for access.

docs.title: Get notified
docs.text: Interested in adopting Data 100? Email ds-help@berkeley.edu and we'll help you get started.

docs.title: Data 6
docs.text:
Data 6, "Introduction to Computational Thinking with Data Science and Society," is an introductory, interdisciplinary course taught at UC Berkeley. It combines statistics, computer science, and social science to give students a practical introduction to computational thinking and exploratory data analysis. The full set of materials is available to adopt.

  • Curriculum and modular adoption: dubois-ctds.github.io
  • Course notes: data6.org/notes
  • Zero to Data 6 guide: data6.org/zero-to-data-6 - context and resources for running Data 6
  • Browse notebooks in your browser: Data 6 xeus-lite
  • Public student materials: dubois-ctds/data-6-materials-student

Quick start

  1. Complete the Data 6 Instructor Interest Form
  2. Fork the student materials
  3. Follow the steps below

docs.title: Data 6: Computational Thinking with Data Science and Society
1. Get access
docs.text:
Create a GitHub account if needed, then complete the Instructor Interest Form (expect a response within ~24 hours). Fork the student materials at dubois-ctds/data-6-materials-student, and accept the invitations to the private solutions repository (dubois-ctds/data-6-materials-solutions) and the Otter Service Standalone org.

Screen recording: forking the student materials

docs.title: 2. Set up Canvas
docs.text: Download the Data 6 Canvas template.

docs.title: Point assignments at your JupyterHub
docs.text:
The default template links assignment URLs to datahub.berkeley.edu. If your institution uses a different JupyterHub, use the Canvas JupyterHub rewriter to update the zip or .imscc file before importing:

  • Upload your Canvas template (zip or .imscc file) to the rewriter
  • Enter your JupyterHub URL (e.g., datahub.berkeley.edu/hub or your institution's hub URL)
  • Enter the materials repository URL: use your fork of the student materials, not the upstream Berkeley repo. Fork dubois-ctds/data-6-materials-student first, then enter your fork URL (e.g., https://github.com/YOUR_GITHUB_USERNAME/data-6-materials-student). For Data 6, fork from https://github.com/dubois-ctds/data-6-materials-student.
  • Scan and preview to see how links will change
  • Confirm that old links are being rewritten to your hub and that the repo points to your fork
  • Rewrite and download the updated file

docs.title: Import into Canvas
docs.text:
We recommend importing the cartridge into a new Canvas course. After the first import, copy that course inside Canvas from term to term instead of re-importing the cartridge into an existing shell (re-importing can duplicate items that do not share the same internal IDs).

  • Create a new course: Settings → Start a new course, name it (e.g., "Data 6")
  • Go to Import Course Content from the course menu
  • Select Common cartridge 1.x package as the content type
  • Upload the .imscc file (the rewritten file from the rewriter step, or the original template if using datahub.berkeley.edu)
  • Upload all content for this initial import
  • Wait for the import: Status will show "queued" then "running" with a progress bar
  • Verify the links. After import, click on an assignment (e.g., Homework 1) and check the link in the bottom-left corner of your browser. It should show your JupyterHub URL. Click through to confirm the notebook loads correctly

What's in the Canvas shell

The Canvas shell includes:

  • Reading assignments embedded as quizzes (ensures student engagement)
  • Week-by-week course structure
  • Pre-configured assignment links
  • Gradebook setup

Other LMS platforms

If you're not using Canvas, the core notebooks are platform-independent and can be linked from any LMS.

docs.title: 3. Student workflow
docs.text:
Here is how students will interact with assignments:

  1. Access: Students log into Canvas and click on an assignment link
  2. Open notebook: The notebook opens in JupyterHub (in a new tab)
  3. Work: Students complete the notebook, checking answers as they go
  4. Export: Students use the "export" cell at the bottom to download their completed notebook
  5. Submit: Students upload the downloaded file to Canvas

docs.title: 4. Grade with Otter
docs.text:
The Otter Service Standalone is a web-based service for grading notebooks, with no local installation required. The service is available at https://grader.datahub.berkeley.edu/.

Screen recording: grading walkthrough (5 min)

Step 1: Authorization. To access the service, we add your GitHub username to our Otter service organization. Once you have been added, accept the invitation by opening the otter-service-stdalone organization, then log in to the grader with your GitHub account.

Step 2: Download your student submissions. Export submissions from Canvas. You can grade a folder of notebooks by compressing it into a zip file and uploading that, or grade one notebook at a time by uploading the notebook itself.

Step 3: Download the solution files (autograder.zip). In the private solutions repository (dubois-ctds/data-6-materials-solutions), browse to the autograder_zips folder and download the autograder.zip for the assignment you are grading. If you do not have access yet, complete the Instructor Interest Form or email ds-help@berkeley.edu.

Step 4: Upload and grade. In the "Grade" section, upload your autograder.zip and the student submissions, then press Grade Notebooks. A "download" code appears under the button. You will need it for the next step.

Step 5: Download your results. The system grades roughly one minute per ten notebooks. When it finishes, enter the code from Step 4 into the "Results" section and a folder with a log file and a CSV file downloads. The CSV contains the grades by notebook; if anything fails, check the log file first.

Version requirement

Data 6 notebooks are configured with otter-grader 6.1.6. If you grade locally, install exactly that version:

pip install otter-grader==6.1.6

docs.title: Instructor grading checklist
docs.text:
1. Download student submissions: Export all submissions from Canvas onto your machine.
  2. Get solutions: Log into the private solutions repository (dubois-ctds/data-6-materials-solutions), navigate to the autograder_zips folder, and download the autograder.zip for the assignment you are grading.
  3. Grade automatically: Log into the grader at https://grader.datahub.berkeley.edu/ with your GitHub username and grade the submissions (see Otter Autograding).
  4. Import grades: Upload the CSV file with grades back to Canvas.

Please do not distribute solutions

Access to dubois-ctds/data-6-materials-solutions is granted on the condition that solutions are not redistributed. This protects the integrity of the course materials for every adopting institution.

docs.title: Course notes
docs.text: The Data 6 course notes serve as lecture notes for the course (source: data-6-berkeley/notes). They complement each topic but should be supplemented with foundational texts linked within the notes.

docs.title: Explore the materials in your browser
docs.text:
The full course calendar is also available as an in-browser notebook preview, with links to slides, worksheets, and videos where available. It runs the notebooks directly in your browser (xeus-lite), so it is handy for previewing the materials. It is not meant for active classroom use, but it is a quick way to explore what the course covers.

Browse Data 6 notebooks in your browser

docs.title: License
docs.text: The Data 6 course notes are licensed under Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC-BY-NC-SA 4.0). Under ShareAlike, if you adapt the notes you must release your version under the same license. See Understanding Licenses for how to read these terms.

docs.title: Acknowledgments
docs.text: This material is based upon work supported by the U.S. National Science Foundation under award Nos. 2245877, 2245878, 2245879, and the California Learning Lab. Learn more about the DUBOIS project at dubois-ctds.github.io.

docs.title: Need help?
docs.text: Email ds-help@berkeley.edu or see Support.

docs.title: Data 8
docs.text:
Data 8, "The Foundations of Data Science," is taught to first-year students at UC Berkeley. It combines inference, computing, and statistics (hypothesis testing, modeling, visualization, and more) into a single introductory course. The full set of materials used at Berkeley is available to adopt.

  • Course site: data8.org · Textbook: inferentialthinking.com
  • Zero to Data 8 guide: data8.org/zero-to-data-8 - syllabus, lectures, course staff, and context for running Data 8
  • Browse notebooks in your browser: Data 8 xeus-lite
  • Public student materials: data-8/materials-fds-v2

Quick start

  1. Complete the Data 8 Instructor Interest Form
  2. Fork the student materials
  3. Follow the steps below

docs.title: Choose your adoption version
docs.text:
Both versions use the same workflow below; they differ mainly in which materials repo and Canvas template you use.

Version 1 Version 2 (recommended) Materials repo materials-fds materials-fds-v2 Basis Spring 2022 track Fall 2025 Curriculum Classic Data 8 sequence Adds multiple linear regression Canvas shell Original template UI + accessibility improvements

New adopters should use Version 2; returning adopters can stay on Version 1 for continuity. The variable values on this page reflect Version 2.

docs.title: 1. Get access
docs.text:
Create a GitHub account if needed, then complete the Instructor Interest Form (indicate which version you'll use). Fork the student materials at data-8/materials-fds-v2, and accept the invitations to the private solutions repository (materials-fds-private) and the Otter Service Standalone org.

Screen recording: forking the student materials

docs.title: 2. Set up Canvas
docs.text: Download the Version 2 Canvas template.

docs.title: Point assignments at your JupyterHub
docs.text:
The default template links assignment URLs to datahub.berkeley.edu. If your institution uses a different JupyterHub, use the Canvas JupyterHub rewriter to update the zip or .imscc file before importing:

  • Upload your Canvas template (zip or .imscc file) to the rewriter
  • Enter your JupyterHub URL (e.g., datahub.berkeley.edu/hub or your institution's hub URL)
  • Enter the materials repository URL: use your fork of the student materials, not the upstream Berkeley repo. Fork data-8/materials-fds-v2 first, then enter your fork URL (e.g., https://github.com/YOUR_GITHUB_USERNAME/materials-fds-v2). For Data 8, fork from https://github.com/data-8/materials-fds-v2.
  • Scan and preview to see how links will change
  • Confirm that old links are being rewritten to your hub and that the repo points to your fork
  • Rewrite and download the updated file

docs.title: Import into Canvas
docs.text:
We recommend importing the cartridge into a new Canvas course. After the first import, copy that course inside Canvas from term to term instead of re-importing the cartridge into an existing shell (re-importing can duplicate items that do not share the same internal IDs).

  • Create a new course: Settings → Start a new course, name it (e.g., "Data 8")
  • Go to Import Course Content from the course menu
  • Select Common cartridge 1.x package as the content type
  • Upload the .imscc file (the rewritten file from the rewriter step, or the original template if using datahub.berkeley.edu)
  • Upload all content for this initial import
  • Wait for the import: Status will show "queued" then "running" with a progress bar
  • Verify the links. After import, click on an assignment (e.g., Homework 1) and check the link in the bottom-left corner of your browser. It should show your JupyterHub URL. Click through to confirm the notebook loads correctly

What's in the Canvas shell

The Canvas shell includes:

  • Reading assignments embedded as quizzes (ensures student engagement)
  • Week-by-week course structure
  • Pre-configured assignment links
  • Gradebook setup

docs.title: 3. Student workflow
docs.text:
Screen recording: student workflow

Here is how students will interact with assignments:

  1. Access: Students log into Canvas and click on an assignment link
  2. Open notebook: The notebook opens in JupyterHub (in a new tab)
  3. Work: Students complete the notebook, checking answers as they go
  4. Export: Students use the "export" cell at the bottom to download their completed notebook
  5. Submit: Students upload the downloaded file to Canvas

docs.title: 4. Grade with Otter
docs.text:
The Otter Service Standalone is a web-based service for grading notebooks, with no local installation required. The service is available at https://grader.datahub.berkeley.edu/.

Screen recording: grading walkthrough (5 min)

Step 1: Authorization. To access the service, we add your GitHub username to our Otter service organization. Once you have been added, accept the invitation by opening the otter-service-stdalone organization, then log in to the grader with your GitHub account.

Step 2: Download your student submissions. Export submissions from Canvas. You can grade a folder of notebooks by compressing it into a zip file and uploading that, or grade one notebook at a time by uploading the notebook itself.

Step 3: Download the solution files (autograder.zip). In the private solutions repository (materials-fds-private), browse to the autograder_zips folder and download the autograder.zip for the assignment you are grading. If you do not have access yet, complete the Instructor Interest Form or email ds-help@berkeley.edu.

Step 4: Upload and grade. In the "Grade" section, upload your autograder.zip and the student submissions, then press Grade Notebooks. A "download" code appears under the button. You will need it for the next step.

Step 5: Download your results. The system grades roughly one minute per ten notebooks. When it finishes, enter the code from Step 4 into the "Results" section and a folder with a log file and a CSV file downloads. The CSV contains the grades by notebook; if anything fails, check the log file first.

docs.title: Instructor grading checklist
docs.text:
1. Download student submissions: Export all submissions from Canvas onto your machine.
  2. Get solutions: Log into the private solutions repository (materials-fds-private), navigate to the autograder_zips folder, and download the autograder.zip for the assignment you are grading.
  3. Grade automatically: Log into the grader at https://grader.datahub.berkeley.edu/ with your GitHub username and grade the submissions (see Otter Autograding).
  4. Import grades: Upload the CSV file with grades back to Canvas.

Please do not distribute solutions

Access to materials-fds-private is granted on the condition that solutions are not redistributed. This protects the integrity of the course materials for every adopting institution.

docs.title: License
docs.text:
Data 8 content is released under two different licenses. Check which applies before you adapt or redistribute:

  • The Data 8 textbook (Inferential Thinking) is licensed CC-BY-NC-ND. You may use it at inferentialthinking.com, but you may not modify or redistribute it without permission from the textbook authors.
  • Data 8 course materials (notebooks, exercises, and other materials) are licensed CC-BY-NC. You are free to modify and distribute them.
  • Private course materials (exams and answers) are kept private to protect course integrity; request access via the instructor interest form.

See Understanding Licenses for how to read these terms.

docs.title: Explore the materials in your browser
docs.text:
The full course calendar is also available as an in-browser notebook preview, with links to slides, worksheets, and videos where available. It runs the notebooks directly in your browser (xeus-lite), so it is handy for previewing the materials. It is not meant for active classroom use, but it is a quick way to explore what the course covers.

Browse Data 8 notebooks in your browser

docs.title: Need help?
docs.text: Email ds-help@berkeley.edu or see Support.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Every course on this site follows the same adoption workflow. Modules have a lighter process (download notebooks and go). This guide is the standardized, single source of truth for the course workflow; each course page then composes these steps with its own repositories, hub, license, and any course-specific notes. For modules, see the Modules section.

Most instructors are up and running in a couple of hours once they have GitHub access and import the Canvas template. If a JupyterHub is being provisioned for you (for example through Cloudbank or Cal-ICOR), that step can take longer depending on scheduling. We will tell you what to expect when you get in touch.

docs.title: How to Adopt
docs.text: The standardized workflow for bringing data science curriculum and infrastructure to your institution.

docs.title: What you'll get
docs.text:
When you adopt a course you can expect:

  • The Jupyter notebooks for labs, homeworks, and projects
  • The solutions to all the notebooks (private)
  • Lecture slides and, where available, videos
  • Worksheets (and solutions) used in discussion/lab sessions
  • A Canvas template customized to use JupyterHub for your institution
  • Access to a JupyterHub instance configured to your campus single sign-on
  • Access to a grading service that grades student notebooks and returns a CSV of grades

docs.title: The workflow at a glance
docs.text:
1. Choose a delivery mode: pick how students will run notebooks (campus JupyterHub, a partner-provisioned hub via Cloudbank or Cal-ICOR, or alternatives like Colab/Binder). See Infrastructure & Tools.
  2. Get access: create a GitHub account, complete the course's instructor interest form, fork the student materials, and accept the solutions + grading invitations.
  3. Set up Canvas: download the course's Canvas template, point its links at your hub, and import it. See Canvas Import.
  4. Grade with Otter: use the web-based Otter Service Standalone (or Gradescope / local). See Otter Autograding.
  5. Adapt content: customize within the bounds of each course's license. See Adapting Content and Understanding Licenses.
  6. Get support: see Support when you need help.

Start with your course or module page

The steps below are for full course adoption. For the exact repository URLs, Canvas template, license, and grader settings, open your course page: Data 6 · Data 8 · Data 100 · ECC CSCI 9. For modules, see Cal-ICOR · Data Science Modules.

docs.title: Adapting Content
docs.text: A major benefit of adopting is that you can customize the materials for your institution, within the bounds of each course's license.

docs.title: Fork, then customize
docs.text:
When you fork a course's public student-materials repository, you get your own copy you can change freely while still pulling updates from the original:

  • Adjust datasets, examples, or context to fit your students
  • Add or remove assignments to match your term length
  • Keep your changes separate from the upstream materials

docs.title: Stay inside the license
docs.text:
What you may modify or redistribute depends on the license attached to each piece of content. Read Understanding Licenses first, then check the license stated on your course page:

  • Content without an ND clause can be modified and redistributed (often with attribution and a non-commercial restriction).
  • Content with an ND (NoDerivatives) clause, such as some textbooks, can be used as-is but not modified or redistributed.

Test before you assign

Run any adapted notebook through the autograder yourself before assigning it. Changing a question can change the expected output the autograder checks against.

docs.title: Otter Autograding
docs.text:
Assignments are Otter-graded Jupyter notebooks. You have three ways to grade them; the Otter Service Standalone web service is the easiest place to start.

For the exact private solutions repository and any pinned otter-grader version, see your course page, Data 8 or Data 6.

docs.title: Option 1: Otter Service Standalone (recommended)
docs.text: A web-based grader at grader.datahub.berkeley.edu, with no local installation. You authorize once with your GitHub username, upload the student submissions plus the assignment's autograder.zip (from your course's private solutions repo), and download a CSV of grades. The full, course-specific steps render on each course page.

docs.title: Option 2: Gradescope
docs.text:
Paid service

Gradescope is a paid service that ties assignments back to Canvas; your institution needs a Gradescope license.

Create the assignment in Canvas and Gradescope, attach the assignment's autograder.zip as the programming autograder, and grades push back to the LMS. See Gradescope's docs on Canvas integration and programming assignments.

docs.title: Option 3: Grade locally
docs.text:
Full control, but requires local setup. Install otter-grader and Docker, then use otter grade.

Match the pinned version

Some courses pin a specific otter-grader version (for example, Data 6 requires 6.1.6). Install exactly the version listed on your course page, or grades may differ.

docs.title: Canvas Import
docs.text:
Each course ships a Canvas common-cartridge template with the week-by-week structure, assignment links, embedded reading quizzes, and gradebook already set up. Importing it is the fastest way to stand up the course.

The steps below are the standardized flow. For the exact Canvas template download and materials repo to fork, open your course page (Data 8 or Data 6), where these same steps render with your course's values.

docs.title: 1. Download the Canvas template
docs.text: Download the common-cartridge (.imscc) template linked from your course page.

docs.title: 2. Point assignments at your JupyterHub
docs.text: By default the template links assignments to datahub.berkeley.edu. If your institution uses a different JupyterHub, run the template through the Canvas JupyterHub rewriter to rewrite the hub URL and your forked materials repository before importing. Fork the student materials repo on your course page first, then enter that fork URL in the rewriter.

docs.title: 3. Import into Canvas
docs.text: Import the cartridge into a new Canvas course, then copy that course term-to-term rather than re-importing (re-importing can duplicate items). After importing, click an assignment and confirm the link points at your JupyterHub.

docs.title: 4. The student workflow
docs.text:
Once Canvas is set up, this is how students interact with assignments:

Here is how students will interact with assignments:

  1. Access: Students log into Canvas and click on an assignment link
  2. Open notebook: The notebook opens in JupyterHub (in a new tab)
  3. Work: Students complete the notebook, checking answers as they go
  4. Export: Students use the "export" cell at the bottom to download their completed notebook
  5. Submit: Students upload the downloaded file to Canvas

Other LMS platforms

Not using Canvas? The core notebooks are platform-independent and can be linked from any LMS. Only the cartridge import is Canvas-specific.

docs.title: Infrastructure & Tools
docs.text:
Decide how your students will run notebooks. The materials on this site are platform-independent Jupyter notebooks, so several options work.

Need a hub without adopting curriculum?

Cal-ICOR and Cloudbank can provision computing infrastructure independently - you don't need to adopt a course or module to get a JupyterHub. For Cal-ICOR, email calicor@berkeley.edu or fill out the interest form. For Cloudbank, email Sean Morris.

docs.title: Campus JupyterHub
docs.text: If your institution already runs a JupyterHub, students sign in with campus single sign-on and notebooks open directly. You'll use the Canvas JupyterHub rewriter to point assignment links at your hub.

docs.title: Cal-ICOR
docs.text: Cal-ICOR (California Interactive Computing Open Resource) provides cloud-hosted computing infrastructure for California public colleges and universities. If your institution is in California and doesn't already run its own hub, Cal-ICOR can provision one configured to your campus sign-on. Provisioning is scheduled, so start early. Email calicor@berkeley.edu or fill out the interest form.

docs.title: Cloudbank
docs.text: Cloudbank is an NSF-funded program that provisions cloud computing infrastructure, including JupyterHubs, for institutions nationwide. If your institution is outside California or needs broader cloud resources, Cloudbank is a good fit. Provisioning is scheduled, so start early.

docs.title: Other options
docs.text: For piloting or smaller deployments, the notebooks also run on Google Colab or Binder. These are good temporary options while a hub is being prepared. Longer term, other platforms such as NRP, Jetstream, Vocareum, and Codespaces may also be supported.

docs.title: What you need
docs.text:
• A GitHub account (free): create one here
  • A way for students to run notebooks (one of the delivery modes above)
  • An LMS such as Canvas if you want to use our cartridge (the notebooks also work with other LMSs)
  • A commitment not to distribute solutions publicly

docs.title: Privacy and accessibility documentation
docs.text: Your institution may need documentation related to privacy (HECVAT) and accessibility (VPAT). The student information required is minimal (an email address). Accessibility is an ongoing effort across UC Berkeley and the Jupyter community; ask us for the current HECVAT and accessibility statements when you adopt.

docs.title: A deeper technical reference
docs.text: For a thorough overview of the infrastructure needed to run a data science course, see The Data Science Educator's Guide to Technical Infrastructure.

docs.title: Understanding Licenses
docs.text:
Adoptable course content is released under Creative Commons licenses, but the exact license differs by course and even by content type within a course (for example, a textbook may be licensed more restrictively than the course notebooks). Always check the license stated on each course's page before you adapt or redistribute its materials.

When you read a Creative Commons license, the letter codes tell you what you may do:

  • BY (Attribution): credit the original authors.
  • NC (NonCommercial): no commercial use.
  • ND (NoDerivatives): you may share it, but not modify or redistribute changes.
  • SA (ShareAlike): if you adapt it, release your version under the same license.

A practical consequence: content marked ND can be used as-is but not modified, while content without ND can be customized for your institution. Per-course license details and links are on each course page.

docs.title: Where each course states its license
docs.text:
Licensing is not standardized across this site. Each course and module (and sometimes each content type within a course) carries its own license. Find the specifics here:

  • Data 8: the textbook and the course materials are licensed differently.
  • Data 6: course notes are licensed under CC-BY-NC-SA 4.0.

Always confirm the license on the course or module page before adapting or redistributing materials.

docs.title: Support
docs.text: We're here to help you adopt successfully.

docs.title: Email us
docs.text: For anything not covered on this site (access issues, hub provisioning, Canvas import errors, or grading questions), email ds-help@berkeley.edu. When reporting an access problem, include the GitHub username you submitted on the interest form (if applicable).

docs.title: Common issues
docs.text:
I can't access the private repositories
  • Confirm you accepted all GitHub invitations (check the bell icon and your email spam folder).
  • Verify your GitHub username matches what you submitted on the interest form.
  • Open the solutions repo and the otter-service-stdalone org while logged in. GitHub often surfaces an "Accept" prompt there.
  • Still stuck after 24 hours? Email ds-help@berkeley.edu.
My JupyterHub isn't ready yet

If your hub is still being provisioned (for example through Cloudbank or Cal-ICOR), we'll give you a realistic timeline. For piloting, consider Google Colab or Binder in the meantime.

Canvas import errors
  • Make sure you're importing a Common cartridge 1.x package.
  • Check Canvas file-size limits.
  • Try importing sections individually if the full import fails.

docs.title: Also see
docs.text:
• FAQ
  • Glossary

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Modules are self-contained notebooks on specific topics (ethnic studies, AI, calculus, chemistry, and more). They are designed to fit into your existing classroom, regardless of discipline.

  • Cal-ICOR Modules

    An inter-segmental effort to bring computational notebooks into courses across California higher education. Instructors can contribute and adopt notebooks.

  • Data Science Modules

    Multidisciplinary Jupyter notebooks developed by UC Berkeley DSUS. Topics span ethnic studies, AI, calculus, chemistry, and more.

docs.title: Modules
docs.text: Standalone Jupyter notebooks you can drop into an existing course - no full course adoption required.

docs.title: Cal-ICOR
docs.text: Cal-ICOR (California Interactive Computing Open Resource) is an effort to bring computational notebooks into courses across California higher education.

docs.title: Materials
docs.text:
• Cal-ICOR Modules Textbook: cal-icor.github.io/textbook - instructions for contributing and adopting notebooks are included in the textbook.
  • Materials share-out: cal-icor.org/materials
  • Calculus resources: github.com/cal-icor/calculus - Jupyter notebooks for instructors teaching Calculus.

docs.title: How to adopt
docs.text: Instructors who use Jupyter notebooks can add their own notebooks to the Cal-ICOR collection. The Cal-ICOR textbook has instructions on how to download and contribute modules.

docs.title: Contact
docs.text: Questions about Cal-ICOR? Email calicor@berkeley.edu or fill out the interest form. You can also reach Cal-ICOR through their contact page.

docs.title: Data Science Modules
docs.text: UC Berkeley Data Science Modules are multidisciplinary Jupyter notebooks that can fit into your classroom regardless of subject area. Topics span ethnic studies, AI, calculus, chemistry, and more.

docs.title: El Camino College x UC Berkeley collaboration
docs.text:
The modules shared here are the result of a collaboration between El Camino College and UC Berkeley's Division of Computing, Data Science, and Society (CDSS). Faculty at El Camino College worked with UC Berkeley's Data Science Undergraduate Studies (DSUS) team to adapt and develop notebooks that bring data science into courses across disciplines. The modules textbook is the primary output of this collaboration, with notebooks covering topics like CalEnviroScreen environmental justice data, AI, calculus, chemistry, and ethnic studies.

This partnership serves as a model for how community colleges and research universities can co-develop open educational resources that work in both settings.

docs.title: Materials
docs.text:
• Data Science Modules: ds-modules.github.io/DS-Modules - the full collection of UC Berkeley Data Science Modules.
  • El Camino College x UC Berkeley Modules Textbook: ds-modules.github.io/ecc-textbook - modules developed through the ECC collaboration, with instructions on how to download and use the notebooks.

docs.title: How to adopt
docs.text: The modules textbook has instructions on how to download the notebooks into your course. Modules are self-contained, so you can pick the ones that fit your curriculum without adopting a full course.

docs.title: Contact
docs.text: Questions about adopting Data Science Modules? Email jedwin321@berkeley.edu.

docs.title: Changelog
docs.text: A record of notable changes to this adoption site.

• New site launched. Migrated from the previous MyST site to MkDocs Material under the ucb-dsus-adopters organization.
  • Standardized adoption guide. Setup, Canvas import, and Otter autograding instructions are now a single source of truth, composed into each course page.
  • Data 8 and Data 6 ported as full adoption packages.
  • El Camino College CSCI 9 (Practical Data Science) and Data 100 added as resource pages while full adoption materials are in progress.
  • Site reframed as curriculum adoption - now covers courses and modules, not just courses.
  • Modules section added with pages for Cal-ICOR and Data Science Modules.
  • Infrastructure page updated - removed tabbed chooser; Cal-ICOR and Cloudbank now have their own sections with clearer descriptions.
  • Glossary updated - expanded Cal-ICOR and Cloudbank entries.

docs.title: FAQ
docs.text:
What does it cost to adopt?

The course and module materials and our support are free. Some optional tools have their own costs. For example, Gradescope is a paid service if you choose it for grading.

How long does adoption take?

Many instructors are running in a couple of hours once they have GitHub access and import the Canvas template. If a JupyterHub is being provisioned for you, that step can take longer depending on scheduling.

Do I have to use Canvas?

No. The notebooks are platform-independent and work with any LMS. Only the common-cartridge template import is Canvas-specific.

Do I need my own JupyterHub?

Not necessarily. You can use a campus hub, a partner-provisioned hub through Cloudbank or Cal-ICOR, or alternatives like Google Colab or Binder. See Infrastructure & Tools.

Can I modify the materials?

Usually yes, though it depends on each course's license. Content without a NoDerivatives (ND) clause can be modified and redistributed. See Understanding Licenses and your course's license section.

How does autograding work?

Assignments are Otter-graded notebooks. The easiest path is the web-based Otter Service Standalone; Gradescope and local grading are also supported. See Otter Autograding.

What do I do if I can't access the private solutions repo?

Confirm you accepted the GitHub invitations and that your username matches the form. See the troubleshooting steps on Support.

Can I get a hub without adopting a course or module?

Yes. Cal-ICOR provisions computing infrastructure for California public colleges and universities (calicor@berkeley.edu), and Cloudbank serves institutions nationwide (Sean Morris). Neither requires you to adopt curriculum from this site. See Infrastructure & Tools.

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text:
Autograder / autograder.zip : The Otter solution bundle for an assignment. You download it from a course's private solutions repository and feed it to the grader along with student submissions.

Canvas common cartridge (.imscc) : A standard package format for importing course content (structure, assignment links, quizzes) into Canvas or another LMS.

Cal-ICOR : California Interactive Computing Open Resource (cal-icor.org), supported by the California Education Learning Lab. Cal-ICOR provides cloud-hosted computing infrastructure (including JupyterHubs) for California public colleges and universities. See also the Cal-ICOR modules page.

Cloudbank : An NSF-funded program (cloudbank.org) that provisions cloud computing infrastructure, including JupyterHubs, for institutions nationwide.

Common cartridge JupyterHub rewriter : A tool that rewrites the hub URLs and materials-repo references inside a Canvas template so assignments point at your JupyterHub before import.

JupyterHub : A multi-user server that gives each student a Jupyter notebook environment in the browser, typically behind campus single sign-on.

LMS (Learning Management System) : The platform that hosts your course (e.g., Canvas) where students access assignments and submit work.

Otter / otter-grader : The autograding framework used to grade Jupyter notebooks. Some courses pin a specific version (e.g., Data 6 requires 6.1.6).

Otter Service Standalone : The web-based Otter grading service at grader.datahub.berkeley.edu, with no local install.

Solutions repository (private) : The private GitHub repo holding solutions and autograder.zip files for a course. Access is granted after completing the instructor interest form.
